vulnerability.title: Disable SSH Support for .rhosts Files
vulnerability.description: SSH can emulate the behavior of the obsolete rsh command in allowing users to enable insecure access to their accounts via .rhosts files. The default SSH configuration disables support for .rhosts. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for IgnoreRhosts. To explicitly disable support for .rhosts files, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: IgnoreRhosts yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complia

triage_decision.reason: Setting IgnoreRhosts yes disables the insecure .rhosts functionality in SSH. This change only affects a legacy, rarely used authentication method and does not interfere with standard SSH authentication, making lockout or service disruption unlikely.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Possible disruption if any legacy automation relies on .rhosts-based authentication (unlikely in modern environments)

commands_executed: sh -c "if ! grep -q \"^IgnoreRhosts yes\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo \"IgnoreRhosts yes\" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi"

execution_details.stdout:
#	$OpenBSD: sshd_config,v 1.104 2021/07/02 05:11:21 dtucker Exp $

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# This sshd was compiled with PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/sbin

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

# To modify the system-wide sshd configuration, create a  *.conf  file under
#  /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/  which will be automatically included below
Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf

# If you want to change the port on a SELinux system, you have to tell
# SELinux about this change.
# semanage port -a -t ssh_port_t -p tcp #PORTNUMBER
#
#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

#PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

#AuthorizedKeysCommand none
#AuthorizedKeysCommandUser nobody

# For this to work you will also need host keys in /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
# HostbasedAuthentication
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
#IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
#PasswordAuthentication yes
#PermitEmptyPasswords no

# Change to no to disable s/key passwords
#KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes

# Kerberos options
#KerberosAuthentication no
#KerberosOrLocalPasswd yes
#KerberosTicketCleanup yes
#KerberosGetAFSToken no
#KerberosUseKuserok yes

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no
#GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes
#GSSAPIStrictAcceptorCheck yes
#GSSAPIKeyExchange no
#GSSAPIEnablek5users no

# Set this to 'yes' to enable PAM authentication, account processing,
# and session processing. If this is enabled, PAM authentication will
# be allowed through the KbdInteractiveAuthentication and
# PasswordAuthentication.  Depending on your PAM configuration,
# PAM authentication via KbdInteractiveAuthentication may bypass
# the setting of "PermitRootLogin without-password".
# If you just want the PAM account and session checks to run without
# PAM authentication, then enable this but set PasswordAuthentication
# and KbdInteractiveAuthentication to 'no'.
# WARNING: 'UsePAM no' is not supported in RHEL and may cause several
# problems.
#UsePAM no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#X11Forwarding no
#X11DisplayOffset 10
#X11UseLocalhost yes
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#Compression delayed
#ClientAliveInterval 0
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	/usr/libexec/openssh/sftp-server

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
#	X11Forwarding no
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to set IgnoreRhosts yes in sshd_config. Likely need to edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config or a file in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/. The remediation snippet suggests creating directory and file. We'll need to ensure line exists: IgnoreRhosts yes. We'll first read the file to see current content, then decide to modify.

We'll use read_file on /etc/ssh/sshd_config. If not exist, maybe default. Let's try.

The file exists. Let's check content for IgnoreRhosts. Probably line commented.

We'll need to ensure line "IgnoreRhosts yes" present and uncommented.

We can use sed to replace or add line. Simplest: ensure line exists. Use sed -i '/^IgnoreRhosts/IgnoreRhosts yes/' file, but if not present we add at end.

Better: Use sed -i '/^#*IgnoreRhosts/d' to uncomment any commented lines, then ensure line exists.

Let's do:

1. Uncomment any IgnoreRhosts line: sed -i '/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//' file.

2. Then ensure line exists: if grep -q '^IgnoreRhosts yes' file; if not, append 'IgnoreRhosts yes'.

We'll need to run these via run_cmd.

Let's construct commands.

First, backup? Not needed.

We'll do:

run_cmd: sed -i '/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

Then run_cmd: if ! grep -q '^IgnoreRhosts yes' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo 'IgnoreRhosts yes' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

We need to use shell condition. run_cmd only accepts one command. We cannot have if/then in single command. We could use a sh -c? But run_cmd expects a single shell command as root. We can embed a small script using sh -c. That's allowed: run_cmd with command: sh -c '...' So we can do sh -c containing multiple statements.

Thus:

First command: run_cmd with command: sh -c 'sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config'

Second command: run_cmd with command: sh -c 'if ! grep -q \"^IgnoreRhosts yes\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo \"IgnoreRhosts yes\" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi'

But we need to ensure proper quoting.

Let's design:

Command1: sh -c 'sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config'

Command2: sh -c 'if ! grep -q \"^IgnoreRhosts yes\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo \"IgnoreRhosts yes\" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi'

We need to escape double quotes inside sh -c. Use single quotes for outer, double inside? Actually sh -c takes a string argument; we can use single quotes for the script and escape inner double quotes with \". So:

sh -c 'sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config'

Here we have double quotes inside, need to escape them: \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" -> we need to escape the double quotes inside the string: use \". Actually outer single quotes, inner double quotes need to be escaped with \". So: sh -c 'sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config' This is ambiguous.

Better: Use double quotes outer and escape inner double quotes with \". So outer double quotes: "sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config". Inside we need to escape the double quotes: \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" . So final: sh -c "sed -i \"/^#*IgnoreRhosts/s/^#//\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config"

Yes.

Second: sh -c "if ! grep -q \"^IgnoreRhosts yes\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo \"IgnoreRhosts yes\" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi"

We need to escape double quotes inside: \". So: sh -c "if ! grep -q \"^IgnoreRhosts yes\" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo \"IgnoreRhosts yes\" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi"

Now produce final JSON array with two function calls.

Let's craft.
We have both commands. Now produce final answer with invocations array.

 Previous attempt tried to modify /etc/ssh/sshd_config directly by uncommenting and setting IgnoreRhosts yes.
 We are now to try a different method: perhaps using the drop-in directory /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/ as suggested in the remediation script.

 The triage context says:

   /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: IgnoreRhosts yes

 However, note that the previous attempt only touched /etc/ssh/sshd_config. We should now try to use the drop-in file.

 We can, however, achieve the same goal by using the drop-in directory and different commands.

 Let's design:

  Step 1: Ensure the drop-in directory exists.
      run_cmd: mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d

  Step 2: Ensure the drop-in file exists with correct permissions.
      run_cmd: touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
      run_cmd: chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

  Step 3: Remove any existing IgnoreRhosts line from the drop-in file (to avoid duplicates) and then add the line.
      We can do: 
        run_cmd: sed -i '/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
        run_cmd: echo "IgnoreRhosts yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

  However, note: we are not allowed to chain commands. So we must run each separately.

  But wait: the previous attempt used two commands and we are not allowed to repeat those exact two. These are different commands and on a different file.

  Alternatively, we can do everything in one sed command? But we are only allowed one command at a time.

  We'll break it into two commands for the drop-in file: one to delete any existing line, and one to append.

  Step 4: We might also want to clean the main config file? The remediation script in the context does that. But to avoid repeating the exact same commands, we can do a different way? 
  However, the previous attempt only touched the main config. We are now touching the drop-in file. So it's a different file.

  Let's proceed.

  We'll do:

    Command 1: mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d
    Command 2: touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    Command 3: chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    Command 4: sed -i '/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
    Command 5: echo "IgnoreRhosts yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

  But note: we are only allowed to run one command at a time. We'll run them sequentially.

  However, we must be cautious: if the file already has the line, we don't want to add duplicates. The sed -i line will remove any existing line, then we append one.

  This should be safe.

  Let's submit the plan for review.

review_feedback: The proposed plan sets IgnoreRhosts to yes, which enables .rhosts support in SSH, thereby introducing the vulnerability (if not already present) or exacerbating it (if present). To resolve the vulnerability, one should set IgnoreRhosts to no or remove the IgnoreRhosts line from the SSH configuration.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed plan sets IgnoreRhosts to yes, which enables .rhosts support in SSH, thereby introducing the vulnerability (if not already present) or exacerbating it (if present). To resolve the vulnerability, one should set IgnoreRhosts to no or remove the IgnoreRhosts line from the SSH configuration.
concerns: The plan enables an insecure authentication method (.rhosts) that SSH should not support by default.
suggested_improvements: Set IgnoreRhosts to no in the SSH configuration to disable .rhosts support.
Remove any existing IgnoreRhosts line from /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf or the main sshd_config file.
